title: Congestion Games, Potential Games, and Price of Anarchy

charts.title: Week 2 Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics in Week 2.

nodes.label:
Week 2
Congestion & Potential Games
nodes.note: Week 2 extends game theory to large structured games. Congestion games model shared-resource conflicts, potential games guarantee equilibrium existence via a potential function, and Price of Anarchy quantifies the efficiency loss from selfish behaviour.

nodes.label:
Motivation:
Concise Games
nodes.note: Representing a general n-player game with k actions per player requires k^n payoff entries. Congestion games avoid this by defining costs through a small set of resource cost functions, enabling scalable reasoning.

nodes.label:
Congestion
Games
nodes.note: A congestion game is defined by players, facilities with congestion-dependent cost functions, and player actions that are subsets of facilities. Each player's cost depends only on how many others use the same resources.

nodes.label: Applications
nodes.note: Congestion games model a wide range of real-world systems including traffic routing, communication networks, cloud computing, multi-robot coordination, energy grids, and IoT sensor networks.

nodes.label:
Braess's
Paradox
nodes.note: Adding a new resource (road) to a network can paradoxically increase the equilibrium travel time for all players. This shows that equilibrium outcomes need not improve when the strategy space expands.

nodes.label:
Best Response
Dynamics
nodes.note: Best response dynamics is an iterative algorithm where players repeatedly switch to their best response. For congestion games, Rosenthal's theorem guarantees this always terminates at a pure Nash equilibrium.

nodes.label:
Potential
Games
nodes.note: A potential game has a single potential function whose changes exactly track the deviating player's cost change. Congestion games are exact potential games, which guarantees the Finite Improvement Property and PSNE existence.

nodes.label:
Max-Cut &
Local Search
nodes.note: The maximum cut problem is NP-hard but solvable to a local optimum via simple local search. Computing local optima of max-cut is PLS-complete, connecting to the hardness of finding PSNE in general potential games.

nodes.label:
Price of Anarchy
& Stability
nodes.note: The Price of Anarchy (PoA) and Price of Stability (PoS) measure the efficiency gap between the worst (or best) Nash equilibrium and the social optimum. For linear congestion games, PoA is at most 5/2.

nodes.label:
Formal Definition
& Network Games
nodes.note: A congestion game is a tuple of players, resources, actions (subsets of resources per player), and cost functions d_j(k) giving the cost of resource j when k players use it.

nodes.label:
Rosenthal's
Theorem
nodes.note: Rosenthal (1973) proved the Finite Improvement Property for congestion games: every sequence of improvement steps is finite. This directly implies that every congestion game has at least one pure Nash equilibrium.

nodes.label:
Rosenthal's
Potential Function
nodes.note: The potential function Phi(a) sums cumulative resource costs across all resources. When a player deviates, the change in Phi equals the change in that player's cost, making it an exact potential function.

nodes.label:
Potential
Game Variants
nodes.note: There are three notions of potential: exact (delta cost = delta Phi), ordinal (signs match), and generalised ordinal (improvement implies Phi decreases). Generalised ordinal potential is equivalent to the Finite Improvement Property.

nodes.label:
Identifying
Potential Games
nodes.note: A game can be tested for the potential property by the guess-and-check method (seed Phi at one profile and derive all others) or by verifying that all unilateral deviation cycles of length 4 sum to zero.

nodes.label:
PoA & PoS
Definitions
nodes.note: PoA is the ratio of the worst Nash equilibrium cost to the optimal cost. PoS is the ratio of the best Nash equilibrium cost to the optimal. Both are at least 1; PoS is always at most PoA.

nodes.label:
Linear Games
PoA Bound 5/2
nodes.note: For congestion games with linear cost functions, the Price of Anarchy is at most 5/2 and this bound is tight. The proof uses the sandwich inequality: half the social cost is at most Phi, which is at most the social cost.

charts.title: Best Response Dynamics Algorithm
charts.description: Iterative algorithm where players switch to best responses; guaranteed to terminate in congestion games.

nodes.label:
Start: arbitrary
action profile a
nodes.note: Initialise with any feasible action profile. In a congestion game, every player is assigned some action (subset of resources) to begin.

nodes.label:
Any player i not
playing best response?
nodes.note: Check whether any player i can reduce their cost by switching to a different action, given everyone else's current choices. If yes, that player is not at a best response.

nodes.label:
Select such
player i
nodes.note: Choose any player who is not currently at a best response. The order of selection can vary; the algorithm is correct regardless of which player moves next.

nodes.label:
Set a_i = best response
to current a_{-i}
nodes.note: Player i switches to the action that minimises their cost given all other players' current actions. This is a strict improvement in cost for player i.

nodes.label:
Phi strictly
decreases
nodes.note: Because the deviation is an improvement and the game is a congestion game, Rosenthal's potential function Phi decreases by the same amount as player i's cost reduction. Since Phi has finitely many values, this ensures termination.

nodes.label:
All players at
best response?
nodes.note: After the update, re-check whether any player still has an improving deviation. If all players are at their best response simultaneously, the current profile is a pure Nash equilibrium.

nodes.label:
PSNE reached
Return a
nodes.note: The algorithm has terminated at a profile where no player can unilaterally reduce their cost. By definition, this is a pure strategy Nash equilibrium of the congestion game.

charts.title: Identifying Potential Games
charts.description: Two methods to test whether a game is a potential game: guess-and-check and cycle inspection.

nodes.label: Start: normal form game
nodes.note: We want to determine whether the game has a potential function. We have two approaches: guess-and-check (build Phi explicitly) or cycle inspection (algebraic test on unilateral deviation cycles).

nodes.label:
Choose approach:
Guess-Check or Cycle?
nodes.note: The guess-and-check method explicitly constructs the potential function. The cycle method algebraically verifies whether a potential can exist without constructing it. Both are equivalent in power.

nodes.label:
Seed: set Phi at
one profile to 0
nodes.note: Assign Phi an arbitrary value (e.g. 0) at any chosen profile. This is valid because potential functions are unique only up to a constant.

nodes.label:
Derive Phi values
via deviation equations
nodes.note: For each unilateral deviation from a known profile, set delta Phi equal to the deviating player's cost change. Propagate these values to all other profiles reachable by single deviations.

nodes.label:
All derived values
consistent?
nodes.note: Check if every profile has a unique, well-defined Phi value regardless of the path of deviations taken to reach it. Inconsistency means different derivation paths give different values for the same profile.

nodes.label:
Form all unilateral
deviation cycles
nodes.note: For a 2-player game, enumerate all cycles of length 4: (a_i, a_j) -> (a'_i, a_j) -> (a'_i, a'_j) -> (a_i, a'_j) -> (a_i, a_j). For each transition, record the deviating player's cost change.

nodes.label:
All cycle sums
equal zero?
nodes.note: A game is a potential game if and only if the sum of cost changes around every unilateral deviation cycle equals zero. A non-zero cycle sum proves no potential function can exist.

nodes.label:
Game IS a
potential game
nodes.note: The game has a valid potential function. Best response dynamics will always terminate, and at least one pure Nash equilibrium is guaranteed to exist.

nodes.label:
Game is NOT a
potential game
nodes.note: No potential function exists for this game. Best response dynamics may cycle and PSNE existence is not guaranteed by this criterion alone.

charts.title: Computing Price of Anarchy and Price of Stability
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure for computing PoA and PoS for a given game.

nodes.label:
Start: game and
optimisation goal
nodes.note: Specify whether the objective is minimisation (e.g. social cost) or maximisation (e.g. social welfare). The PoA and PoS formulas differ between the two.

nodes.label:
Find optimal
strategy profile s*
nodes.note: Compute or enumerate all strategy profiles and find the one with the best social objective (minimum social cost or maximum social welfare). This is the benchmark.

nodes.label:
Find all pure
Nash equilibria
nodes.note: Enumerate or compute all strategy profiles where no player has an incentive to deviate. For congestion and potential games, at least one PSNE is guaranteed by Rosenthal's theorem.

nodes.label:
Any Nash equilibrium
found?
nodes.note: If no Nash equilibrium exists (possible in general games without mixed strategies), PoA and PoS are undefined. In congestion games, a PSNE always exists.

nodes.label:
Compute social objective
for each NE
nodes.note: For each identified Nash equilibrium, calculate the social cost (sum of costs) or social welfare (sum of utilities) to determine how efficient each equilibrium is.

nodes.label:
PoA = worst NE value
divided by opt value
nodes.note: For minimisation: PoA is the maximum social cost over all NE divided by the optimal social cost. For maximisation: PoA is the optimal welfare divided by the minimum welfare over all NE. Always at least 1.

nodes.label:
PoS = best NE value
divided by opt value
nodes.note: For minimisation: PoS is the minimum social cost over all NE divided by the optimal social cost. PoS measures whether a good equilibrium exists. Always between 1 and PoA.

nodes.label: PoA and PoS computed
nodes.note: PoA and PoS quantify equilibrium inefficiency. PoA = 1 means all equilibria are optimal; PoS = 1 means at least one equilibrium is optimal.

nodes.label:
PoA/PoS undefined
for this game
nodes.note: Without any Nash equilibrium, the efficiency measures cannot be defined. Consider mixed strategy equilibria or a different solution concept.

charts.title: Potential Game Types Hierarchy
charts.description: Classification of potential game variants from strongest to weakest, with their properties.

nodes.label:
Potential Game
Types
nodes.note: Potential games are classified by how closely the potential function tracks individual cost changes. Stronger notions give stronger guarantees about convergence and equilibrium.

nodes.label:
Exact Potential Game
delta cost = delta Phi
nodes.note: The change in the potential function equals exactly the change in the deviating player's cost for every unilateral deviation. Congestion games are always exact potential games. Implies FIP and PSNE existence.

nodes.label:
Ordinal Potential Game
signs of changes match
nodes.note: The sign of the potential change matches the sign of the cost change: a cost improvement implies a Phi decrease and vice versa. Weaker than exact, but still implies FIP and PSNE existence.

nodes.label:
Generalised Ordinal
cost down implies Phi down
nodes.note: Every cost improvement implies a Phi decrease, but a Phi decrease need not imply a cost improvement. This is the weakest potential notion and is equivalent to the Finite Improvement Property.

nodes.label:
Finite Improvement
Property (FIP)
nodes.note: Every sequence of improvement steps is finite. This is equivalent to having a generalised ordinal potential function. FIP directly implies that best response dynamics always terminates.

nodes.label:
PSNE Existence
guaranteed
nodes.note: All games with FIP (and hence all potential games) have at least one pure strategy Nash equilibrium. The terminal state of best response dynamics is always a PSNE.

charts.title: Braess's Paradox: Adding a Road Hurts
charts.description: How adding a zero-cost shortcut between two roads can increase equilibrium travel time for all players.

nodes.label:
Start: network with
two parallel routes
nodes.note: 200 travelers go from A to D. Route 1: A to B (cost K/10) then B to D (cost 20). Route 2: A to C (cost 20) then C to D (cost K/10). K is the number of users on that edge.

nodes.label:
Find NE without
shortcut B-C
nodes.note: With only two routes and no shortcut, the unique Nash equilibrium has 100 travelers on each route. Each traveler's time is 100/10 + 20 = 30 minutes.

nodes.label:
Add free shortcut
B to C (cost 0)
nodes.note: Introduce a zero-cost road directly from B to C. This creates a new strategy: travel A to B to C to D, using only the two congestion-dependent edges.

nodes.label:
Does any traveler
benefit by deviating?
nodes.note: With 100 on each original route, a traveler switching to A-B-C-D would pay 101/10 + 0 + 100/10 = 20.1 minutes, which is less than 30. So yes, deviation is profitable.

nodes.label:
All travelers shift
to A-B-C-D route
nodes.note: Each traveler individually finds A-B-C-D attractive, causing a cascade of switches. At the new equilibrium, all 200 take A-B-C-D.

nodes.label:
New NE: all 200
on A-B-C-D route
nodes.note: All 200 travelers use A to B to C to D. The cost is 200/10 + 0 + 200/10 = 40 minutes each. No traveler can improve by switching: all alternatives also cost 40 minutes.

nodes.label:
Travel time INCREASED
30 min to 40 min
nodes.note: The free shortcut worsened the equilibrium for everyone: travel time rose from 30 to 40 minutes. This illustrates that selfish routing at equilibrium can be made worse by expanding the strategy space.

nodes.label:
Original NE is stable
30 min per traveler
nodes.note: Without the shortcut, the 100/100 split is a stable Nash equilibrium at 30 minutes. It is both the NE and the social optimum in this network.
